questions.question: Explain why String is immutable in Java. List at least three advantages of String immutability.
questions.hint: Consider aspects like security, thread safety, memory management, and performance
keyPoints: String is immutable - its value cannot be changed after creation
Advantage 1: Security - Strings are used for sensitive data like database credentials, URLs
Advantage 2: Thread safety - Immutable objects can be shared across threads without synchronization
Advantage 3: String pool optimization - JVM can reuse string literals saving memory
Advantage 4: Hashcode caching - String can cache its hashcode since it's immutable, improving HashMap performance
Advantage 5: Classloader security - Immutable strings prevent security issues during class loading

questions.question: Compare StringBuilder and StringBuffer in Java. When would you use each?
questions.hint: Focus on thread-synchronization and performance differences
keyPoints: StringBuilder is non-synchronized (not thread-safe), StringBuffer is synchronized (thread-safe)
StringBuilder provides better performance than StringBuffer in single-threaded environment
StringBuffer should be used in multi-threaded applications where thread safety is required
StringBuilder was introduced in JDK 1.5, StringBuffer was available since JDK 1.0
Both provide same API and functionality except for synchronization

questions.question: What is the difference between the + operator for string concatenation and the concat() method?
questions.hint: Consider behavior with null values and efficiency in loops
keyPoints: The + operator can concatenate any data type with strings (automatic toString conversion)
The concat() method only accepts String objects
The + operator creates multiple intermediate String objects in a loop (inefficient)
The concat() method is more efficient for joining two strings directly
The + operator uses StringBuilder internally in JDK 5+
The concat() method throws NullPointerException if argument is null

questions.question: Explain the substring() method in String class. What is the time complexity of this operation?
questions.hint: Consider the memory leak issue in JDK 6
keyPoints: substring(int beginIndex) - returns string from beginIndex to end
substring(int beginIndex, int endIndex) - returns string from beginIndex to endIndex-1
Both methods return a new String object
In JDK 7+, a new character array is created (safe but uses more memory)
In JDK 6, the same underlying char array was shared (caused memory leak issues)
Time complexity is O(n) where n is the length of substring due to character copying

questions.question: Explain the difference between replace(), replaceFirst(), and replaceAll() methods in String class.
questions.hint: Focus on the regex aspect of replaceAll and replaceFirst
keyPoints: replace(char oldChar, char newChar) - replaces all occurrences of a specific character
replace(CharSequence target, CharSequence replacement) - replaces all occurrences of sequence
replaceFirst(String regex, String replacement) - replaces only the first match using regex
replaceAll(String regex, String replacement) - replaces all matches using regex
replaceAll and replaceFirst treat the first argument as a regular expression
replace does not use regex (simpler, faster for literal replacements)

questions.question: What is the difference between trim() and strip() methods in Java String?
questions.hint: Consider Unicode awareness and Java version
keyPoints: trim() removes leading and trailing ASCII space characters (Unicode U+0020)
strip() removes leading and trailing Unicode whitespace (all Unicode whitespace characters)
strip() was introduced in Java 11
strip() is Unicode-aware and follows Unicode standard
strip() handles all Unicode whitespace including non-breaking spaces
For ASCII text, both methods work similarly

questions.question: Explain various methods available in Java for modifying strings. Demonstrate with code examples for each method.
questions.hint: Provide code examples for each method
keyPoints: concat(String str) - joins two strings
substring(int start, int end) - extracts portion of string
replace(char/String old, char/String new) - replaces characters or sequences
replaceFirst(String regex, String replacement) - replaces first match
replaceAll(String regex, String replacement) - replaces all matches
trim() - removes leading/trailing spaces
strip() - removes Unicode whitespace (Java 11+)
toLowerCase() and toUpperCase() - changes case
intern() - returns string from pool
All methods (except intern) return new String objects due to immutability

questions.question: Discuss the performance implications of string concatenation in loops. Compare different approaches with examples.
questions.hint: Explain why StringBuilder is preferred in loops
keyPoints: Using + operator in loop creates many intermediate String objects (O(n²) time complexity)
Each iteration creates new String object, copying all previous characters
Approach 1: StringBuilder - mutable, single buffer, O(n) time complexity
Approach 2: StringBuffer - same as StringBuilder but synchronized
Approach 3: String.concat() - better than + for two strings but still creates new object
Example code showing loop with StringBuilder vs + operator
StringBuilder should be used when modifying strings repeatedly
For compilation, JDK 5+ converts + in loops to StringBuilder automatically (but in loop body, not for concatenation)

questions.question: Design a Java program that counts the number of vowels, consonants, and spaces in a given string using StringBuilder for the result.
questions.hint: Use Character class methods for classification
keyPoints: Initialize StringBuilder with input string
Iterate through each character of the string
Check if character is vowel (a, e, i, o, u) - case insensitive
Check if character is consonant (alphabetic but not vowel)
Check if character is space
Maintain separate counters for vowels, consonants, spaces
Use Character.isLetter() and Character.isWhitespace() methods
Display final counts

questions.question: Explain the internal working of StringBuilder. How does it manage memory and grow dynamically?
questions.hint: Focus on the internal char array and capacity management
keyPoints: StringBuilder uses a char array (value) to store characters internally
Initial capacity is 16 if not specified, or (oldCapacity * 2) + 2 if not specified
When capacity is exceeded, it creates a new larger array
The grow() method doubles capacity plus 2
append() method copies characters to the internal array
StringBuilder is not synchronized (no thread safety overhead)
toString() creates a new String with the current content
StringBuilder is more efficient than String for multiple modifications

questions.hint: Remember String is immutable - concat() returns a new String
keyPoints: s1 remains unchanged - prints "Java" (immutability)
s2 contains the concatenated result - prints "Java Programming"
s1 == s2 compares references, not content
s1 and s2 are different objects in memory, so == returns false
To compare content, one should use equals() method

questions.hint: Track each operation step by step
keyPoints: After append: "Hello World"
After insert(5, ","): "Hello, World" (inserts at index 5)
After replace(6, 11, "Java"): "Hello, Java" (replaces positions 6-10)
After reverse(): "avaJ ,olleH"
Final output: "avaJ ,olleH"
toString() creates a new String from StringBuilder content
Default initial capacity: 16
After first append("university") - length 3, capacity remains 16
After second append(" Advanced Java") - length 16 (3+13), capacity 16
After third append(" Semester 6") - length 27 (16+11), capacity exceeds, new capacity = 16*2+2 = 34
After fourth append(" Module 2") - length 36 (27+9), capacity 34
Final length: 36
Final capacity: 34 (or more if exceeded again during last append)

questions.hint: Note the difference between stripLeading and stripTrailing
keyPoints: Original string has leading and trailing spaces
trim() removes ASCII spaces only - output: [Welcome to Java Programming]
strip() removes Unicode whitespace - same as trim() for ASCII spaces
stripLeading() removes only leading whitespace - output: [Welcome to Java Programming ]
stripTrailing() removes only trailing whitespace - output: [ Welcome to Java Programming]
All methods return new strings, original string remains unchanged
